summary: Jeff Atwood explores the broken window theory and its application to software development, drawing from the original 1982 Atlantic Monthly article and the Pragmatic Programmers' adaptation. He examines Philip Zimbardo's famous experiments showing how abandoned cars in both the Bronx and Palo Alto were eventually vandalized once initial signs of neglect appeared. The post argues that the mere perception of disorder triggers a negative feedback loop that can lead to complete system breakdown. Atwood concludes that programmers must sweat the small stuff because seemingly minor issues signal that no one cares, accelerating decay.
key_points: Neglect accelerates software rot faster than any other factor - leaving 'broken windows' unrepaired signals that no one cares about the codebase
The perception of disorder, not just actual disorder, triggers the negative feedback loop that leads to system breakdown
Even 'respectable' people will participate in destruction once communal barriers and the sense of mutual regard are lowered
The broken window effect applies universally - it happened in wealthy Palo Alto just as it did in the Bronx, once the initial damage occurred
Programming's extreme detail-orientation exists because losing control of small things creates the perception that everything is out of control
money_quotes: One unrepaired broken window is a signal that no one cares, and so breaking more windows costs nothing.
Neglect accelerates the rot faster than any other factor.
Untended property becomes fair game for people out for fun or plunder and even for people who ordinarily would not dream of doing such things.
The car in Palo Alto sat untouched for more than a week. Then Zimbardo smashed part of it with a sledgehammer. Soon, passersby were joining in.
If you're not on top of the details, the perception is that things are out of control, and it's only a matter of time before your project spins out of control.
Maybe we should be sweating the small stuff.
Don't leave broken windows unrepaired. Fix each one as soon as it is discovered.
key_insight: Small signs of neglect in code signal that no one cares, triggering a psychological feedback loop where further degradation becomes acceptable and inevitable.
